children.text: Eightieth session – Agenda item 154 – Financing of the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo – Budget performance for the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and budget for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 for the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo – Report of the Secretary-General

Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions

The present report contains the budget performance and the budget proposal for the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo (UNMIK) for the periods from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025 and 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, respectively.

During the 2024/25 period, UNMIK continued to implement its mandate with the overall objective of promoting security, stability and respect for human rights in Kosovo and the region, ensuring conditions for a peaceful and normal life for all inhabitants of Kosovo. UNMIK supported the strengthening of peace, security and stability in Kosovo by: (a) having contributed to the implementation of agreements reached between Belgrade and Pristina in the framework of the European Union-facilitated dialogue; (b) further promoting intercommunity trust-building; (c) providing support in the areas of human rights and the rule of law; (d) fulfilling its facilitation and liaison roles; and (e) monitoring and reporting on developments.

During the 2026/27 period, UNMIK will build on its achievements of the past years by continuing to focus on its strategic priority areas, including utilizing its good offices, as well as its monitoring and reporting functions and programmatic activities. It will support progress towards the normalization of relations between Belgrade and Pristina; promote intercommunity trust-building; provide support in the areas of human rights and the rule of law; fulfil its coordination and facilitation roles; and advance the women and peace and security agenda and the youth, peace and security agenda.

Financial resources

	In 2024/25, the Mission incurred $43.7 million in expenditure for the reporting period, representing a resource utilization rate of 100 per cent, compared with $40.6 million in expenditure and a resource utilization rate of 99.9 per cent in 2023/24.

The unencumbered balance of $25,100 reflects the net impact of: (a) increased requirements for civilian personnel, attributable primarily to a lower actual vacancy rate than the rate applied in the approved budget for international staff; and (b) reduced requirements for operational costs, attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to meet the increased requirements for civilian personnel.
The budget proposed for 2026/27 in the amount of $42.0 million represents a decrease of $4.0 million, or 8.6 per cent, compared with the apportionment of $46.0 million approved for 2025/26.

The reduced requirements for 2026/27 are attributable primarily to the lower requirements for civilian personnel, owing to the proposed net reduction of 21 international and 28 national staff, and the application of the higher vacancy rates for national staff, offset in part by the higher salary rates for national staff.
(Thousands of United States dollars; budget year is from 1 July to 30 June)

Human resourcesa

	The proposed budget provides for the deployment of uniformed personnel and civilian staff as follows:

a		Represents the highest level of approved/proposed strength.
	b		Includes international United Nations Volunteers.

Legislative history
(Thousands of United States dollars)

The actions to be taken by the General Assembly are set out in section IV of the present report.

text: I.	Mandate, results achieved and planned outcome

children.text: A.	Mandate

1.	The mandate of UNMIK was established by the Security Council in its resolution 1244 (1999). The Mission is mandated by the Security Council to achieve an overall objective, namely, to ensure conditions for a peaceful and normal life for all inhabitants in Kosovo and advance regional stability in the Western Balkans.
B.	Budget implementation for 2024/25

2.	During the performance period, UNMIK continued to work towards the fulfilment of its strategic objective of strengthening and consolidating peace, security and stability in Kosovo and the region. This included contributing towards the implementation of agreements reached between Belgrade and Pristina within the framework of the European Union-facilitated dialogue, promoting intercommunity trust-building, and human rights, providing support in the area of the rule of law and fulfilling its coordination and facilitation roles. In implementing those priorities, the Mission ensured the integration of gender and youth perspectives into all aspects of its work, in accordance with Security Council resolutions 1325 (2000) and 2250 (2015) and subsequent resolutions on women and peace and security and on youth, peace and security, respectively. UNMIK also continued to conduct political analysis and reporting.

3.	The reporting period was marked by the legislative elections held in February 2025, with the subsequent inability of political parties to form a coalition government and constitute the Assembly of Kosovo. The elections proceeded peacefully without major incidents. However, thereafter, Kosovo endured a prolonged institutional stalemate following the certification of the results in March 2025. Repeated attempts to elect the President of the Assembly as part of the process to constitute the new legislature failed due to persistent disagreements among political parties and insufficient support for the nominated candidate. That impasse contributed to a delay in forming a new government and stalled key reforms, including the ratification of agreements required for Kosovo to access funding under the European Union Growth Plan for the Western Balkans.

4.	During the 2024/25 period, the Mission continued to capture its impact through the Comprehensive Planning and Performance Assessment System, working towards strengthening the effectiveness of its operations. In addition, the Mission continued to contribute towards the implementation of the Secretary-General’s Action for Peacekeeping Plus strategy.

5.	Out of the total 59 projects implemented, 3 were implemented by UNMIK, while 56 were implemented solely by external implementing partners. Regarding the latter, 4 were implemented by the United Nations Kosovo team and 52 were implemented by civil society organizations and non-governmental organizations. In addition to providing support to community stabilization, the projects covered the following thematic areas:

(a)	Rule of law: seven projects were implemented in this area. The Mission continued to support access to justice in northern Kosovo and language rights through the implementation of two projects: one that provided three translators and three legal associates to the Basic Court of Mitrovica to reduce case backlog and promote the integration of lawyers from non-majority communities through the provision of trainings; and another that engaged two legal officers and two translators from non-majority communities in the Basic Prosecution Office in Mitrovica to support translation. The third project supported the enforcement of property legislation in Kosovo by raising awareness on property rights and registration processes. The fourth project supported the Association of Women in the Kosovo Police by enhancing career development prospects for female police officers and delivering capacity-building training on gender-responsive and community-oriented policing. The fifth project raised awareness about combating corruption through the organization of a regional conference. The sixth project supported access to justice for all communities and strengthened transparency within the justice system by providing free legal aid services for 316 residents from all communities in the legal aid centres of Pristina and North Mitrovica, funded by the Mission, as well as providing support for the monitoring of 238 court hearings delivered in Serbian on civil and criminal matters in all Kosovo courts. The seventh project enhanced the capacity of the Kosovo Forensic Agency to deliver services to law enforcement agencies by supplying it with a forensic information technology workstation;

(b)	Promotion and protection of human rights: six projects implemented in this area also provided support in the area of the rule of law. One project enhanced the capacity of local institutions to support the rights of persons with disabilities. The second project tackled public awareness of human rights violations, while the third project supported the Civil Society Human Rights Network and students at the University of Pristina through the delivery of a master’s degree programme on human rights. The fourth project focused on enhancing the human rights monitoring and reporting capacities of civil society organizations. The fifth project contributed towards the effective and inclusive implementation of the Kosovo transitional justice strategy, supporting services to survivors of conflict-related sexual violence and the families of missing persons. The sixth project enhanced the capacities of central and local authorities in promoting and protecting language rights, as mandated by the Law on the Use of Languages, fostering integration and combating discrimination against non-majority communities across Kosovo;
(c)	Community stabilization was advanced through the implementation of 36 projects, comprising 23 community trust-building projects and 13 projects focused on community stabilization, as follows:

(i)	Community trust-building was promoted through 23 projects, including 2 projects that supported inter-ethnic cooperation and reconciliation, inter-ethnic dialogue and language rights, and also aimed at countering hate speech; 2 projects were focused on the rights, inclusion and well-being of people with disabilities; 6 projects promoted community trust-building and contributed to women’s empowerment; 1 project was related to environmental awareness; 2 projects encouraged cooperation among entrepreneurs from different communities; 4 projects supported intercultural dialogue; 2 projects promoted youth empowerment and community cohesion through sports; 2 projects provided capacity-building and on-the-job training; 1 project focused on the role of media in promoting social cohesion; and 1 project supported students of schools with mixed communities;

(ii)	The 13 community stabilization projects also contributed towards the promotion of intercommunity trust-building. Of those 13 projects, 4 strategic communication projects were implemented as follows: 1 project aimed at promoting cross-community engagement through participation in the 2024 “Mitrovica International Jazz Days”; 1 project aimed at enhancing inter-ethnic cooperation by changing conflictual media narratives and promoting intercommunity dialogue; 1 project supported the training of journalists from different communities in identifying and reporting hate speech; and 1 project helped to build the professional capacities of Kosovo-Roma and Ashkali young people on journalism, videography and video production through training and internships in the mainstream media. Of the other nine projects, one project supported the Barabar Centre to enhance direct contact and exchange between communities in Kosovo and strengthen cross-community networks; two projects aimed at fostering multi-ethnic cooperation by supporting women entrepreneurs and through artistic and cultural exchanges; one project supported multi-ethnic cooperation by bringing together civil society organizations from different communities to work on environmental protection; one project supported cultural exchanges among young people from different ethnic backgrounds; one project supported multimedia campaigns and video production on grass-roots trust-building initiatives, enhancing mutual understanding and tolerance among all communities; one project involved the organization of multi-ethnic forums enabling open discussion on the challenges linked to the security context in northern Kosovo; and two projects helped to address the problem of hate speech and empowered members of the Kosovo Roma, Ashkali and Egyptian communities through training and internships;

(d)	The women and peace and security agenda was advanced through the implementation of eight projects. One project aimed at advancing gender perspectives and enhancing women’s inclusion in the normalization process by organizing two events with representatives from civil society, political parties and local institutions in Belgrade and Pristina. Two projects helped to address gender-based violence, including one that raised public awareness on the issue by promoting prevention and community engagement, and another that helped to build the capacity of judicial and legal professionals from the region to respond effectively to gender-based violence cases. A further two projects were focused on empowering women and promoting gender equality, including one that supported an exhibition on women’s leadership at the Barabar Centre and another that supported the organization of events highlighting the role of women in sports, leadership and entrepreneurship. One further project provided vocational training and psychosocial support to women from non-majority and marginalized communities, fostering inter-ethnic dialogue and economic empowerment. Another project built the capacities of women and men potential candidates to navigate electoral challenges, including men’s understanding of gender-responsive electoral systems and their role in supporting inclusive political participation. Another project established and implemented a mentorship programme for 27 female students from diverse communities at the University of Pristina. The programme supported professional growth, leadership development and career advancement and supported the organization of the Global Open Day on Women, Peace and Security, which brought together representatives from women’s civil society organizations, central and local government institutions and the international community;

(e)	The youth, peace and security agenda was advanced through two projects. The first project involved organizing an end-of-year support event for children from different communities without parental care, providing clothing, stationery and books. The second project supported the implementation of the UNMIK framework strategy on youth, peace. It also supported security through a series of impactful activities, including the organization of the seventh United Nations Youth Assembly, which brought together over 130 young people from diverse communities to discuss their role in promoting peace, inclusion and resilience; the promotion of local ownership of the youth, peace and security agenda by supporting five advocacy workshops across the municipalities of Lipjan/Lipljan, Peja/Peć, Prizren, Gjilan/ Gnjilane and North Mitrovica. The project also supported the implementation of four small-scale, youth-led initiatives aimed at fostering cross-community cooperation.
C.	Planning assumptions and mission support initiatives for 2026/27

6.	During the 2026/27 period, UNMIK will build on its achievements of previous years in its strategic priority areas, namely: using its good offices, monitoring and reporting functions and programmatic activities to support progress towards the normalization of relations between Belgrade and Pristina; promoting intercommunity trust-building; providing support in the areas of human rights and rule of law; fulfilling its coordination and facilitation roles; and advancing the women and peace and security agenda and the youth, peace and security agenda.

7.	Guided by the Secretary-General’s Action for Peacekeeping Plus initiative, UNMIK will continue to prioritize work in support of the dialogue process, provide support in the areas of human rights and the rule of law, and provide intercommunity trust-building initiatives by promoting the role of community leaders, women, young people and other change-makers and their participation in local governance structures.

8.	The Mission will work towards fostering trust among communities and between communities and institutions, by supporting central and local level institutions, civil society organizations and other relevant stakeholders. Strategic emphasis will be placed on tangible, community-driven initiatives that reflect the needs of all communities, especially non-majority communities. The Mission will actively engage with community leaders, civil society, local and central authorities and other relevant stakeholders to strengthen social cohesion, with a strong emphasis on grass-roots, locally led initiatives that bring communities together, addressing grievances and fostering peaceful coexistence.

9.	The Mission will continually optimize its programmatic activities based on lessons learned, while looking for new avenues for innovation and entry points. Specific focus will be placed on collaborative initiatives of shared interest, such as economic development through the empowerment of women, youth and non-majority communities; housing, land and property rights; the protection of cultural heritage; and the promotion of arts and culture, which offer inclusive and neutral platforms for dialogue, cooperation and the cultivation of shared values across community divides.

10.	The Mission will continue to support the promotion and protection of human rights and the strengthening of the rule of law. It will continue its active engagement with the Kosovo authorities, the Ombudsperson Institution and civil society for enhanced compliance with international and regional human rights standards, strengthened civic space, increased awareness through human rights education, and continued engagement with human rights mechanisms. The Mission will continue to support the European Union-facilitated efforts towards judicial integration, enhancing efforts towards equal access to justice and ensuring language rights for all communities in Kosovo. The Mission will continue to monitor, analyse and report on rule of law developments, including possible progress on the reintegration of Kosovo-Serbs into judicial institutions in northern Kosovo. Its activities will include regular field visits and consultative meetings at prosecution offices, correctional institutions and other law enforcement entities, monitoring justice and law enforcement institutions, legislative developments and drafting thematic reports, including those in the context of monthly meetings of the Rule of Law Civil Society Coordination Committee convened by UNMIK in northern Kosovo.

11.	UNMIK will continue to perform functions related to the certification of civil status documents and liaise with the International Criminal Police Organization (INTERPOL). It will facilitate, where necessary, the participation of Kosovo representatives in international meetings, particularly those organized or co-organized by the United Nations system. The Mission will also continue to fulfil its commitment to support progress on the determination of the fate of missing persons by maintaining its support of the Working Group on persons who are unaccounted for in connection with the events in Kosovo, through liaison with the Kosovo Commission on Missing Persons and the Serbian Government Commission on Missing Persons and by facilitating meetings of families of missing persons.

12.	The wide circulation of mis- and disinformation, especially on social media, continues to undermine trust among Kosovo communities and between communities and authorities. The Mission will continue to use its strategic communication tools and resources to help address these challenges. The Mission will maintain its partnerships with civil society, relevant institutions and United Nations entities to address mis- and disinformation to support narratives that promote peaceful coexistence and contribute to sustained peace for all residents in Kosovo. UNMIK will also continue to ensure the visibility of its activities through: (a) creative storytelling in line with the Secretary-General’s Action for Peacekeeping Plus strategic communication priorities and the United Nations global communications strategy; and (b) an expanded use of the Mission’s website and other social media platforms, with solution-focused and human-centric storytelling that conveys messages that foster community cohesion.

13.	In the delivery of its mandated activities, UNMIK will ensure continued collaboration with the United Nations Kosovo team, based on common priorities and implementation mechanisms, and in line with the complementarity envisioned in the UNMIK-United Nations Kosovo team integrated strategic framework.

14.	Building on the experiences in 2025/26, the Mission proposes six quick-impact projects for 2026/27, with a total budget of $300,000. As was the case in 2025/26, the projects would be small-scale and low-cost, while continuing to complement the Mission’s ongoing programmatic activities.
Mission support initiatives

15.	The Mission support component will continue to successfully provide effective and efficient logistical, administrative and technical services necessary for the implementation of its mandate through the delivery of related outputs, service improvements and efficiency gains.

16.	UNMIK will continue its commitment to reducing its environmental footprint. The Mission will continue to maintain its renewable energy capability with the installed solar-powered system at Mission headquarters in Pristina and repeater sites in the region. Furthermore, the Mission will continue to implement a range of environmental initiatives aimed at reducing its environmental and carbon footprint, as set out in the Mission-wide environmental action plan and its 2025–2027 water conservation programme and waste management plan. During the 2026/27 period, the Mission will continue to maintain the environmental management system certified under ISO 14001:2015, which will contribute to improved energy efficiency, waste reduction and water conservation.

17.	Given the challenging financial environment, the 2026/27 budget proposed by UNMIK underpins the most critical needs to support mandate implementation and to enable UNMIK to be responsive to evolving operational realities. The outcome of the civilian staffing review led to the restructuring and consolidation of functions, resulting in the proposed civilian staffing component and significant reduction of posts. To ensure the safety and security of the Mission’s civilian and uniformed personnel, UNMIK regularly reviews contingency plans and security risk management and related policy documents. These reviews are conducted in line with the security plan and security risk management policy of the United Nations security management system in Kosovo, as overseen by the United Nations Security Management Team.

18.	In line with the Secretary-General’s UN80 Initiative, the proposed budget reflects targeted efficiency measures through organizational optimization, functional streamlining and improved alignment of resources with priorities. UNMIK proposes to make adjustments to personnel, including reduced deployment through a higher vacancy rate for military observers and United Nations police officers; and the restructuring of staffing structures, including the abolishment of posts, in accordance with the recommendations of the civilian staffing review, as further explained in the section below. Those adjustments reflect the prioritization of operational requirements and optimized deployment, enabling the effective delivery of mandated activities with a leaner footprint.

19.	The above efficiency measures will be supported by streamlined organizational structures, including the redistribution of responsibilities and simplified workflows, strengthened coordination between uniformed and civilian components and enhanced utilization of shared services through the Regional Service Centre in Entebbe, Uganda (RSCE).

20.	While flexibility to respond to peaks in demand may be more constrained, the Mission will continue to apply risk-based prioritization and adaptive planning and strengthen management oversight to mitigate any potential risks. The impact of the measures will be closely monitored and adjusted as necessary to sustain effective mandate implementation.
Civilian staffing review

21.	During the 2025/26 period, a civilian staffing review of UNMIK was undertaken in line with General Assembly resolution 76/274. As part of the review, structures and functions were assessed, with a focus on operational priorities, and opportunities identified to enhance coordination and streamline roles within and between components. The review was strongly guided by the maximizing of efficiencies, and its recommendations therefore sought to optimize the use of existing capacities, including through enhanced integration and communication, and to improve performance with a reduced footprint.

22.	The review also included an assessment of the existing structure and staffing, in alignment with the Department for Operational Support guidelines on mission support structure for small missions and with budgetary directives. The recommendations included that the staffing structure be more strategically aligned and capability-driven to strengthen Mission support, enhance integration across civilian components and address key operational gaps. Civilian staffing was comprehensively assessed across all functions, with a focus on capacity, localization and non-location-dependent roles. Opportunities were identified for efficiency, upskilling and reducing duplication, while exploring shared services and collaboration with the United Nations Kosovo team. The key recommendations included streamlining the top-heavy structure, regularizing loaned posts, restructuring Mission support, streamlining functions, clarifying responsibilities and consolidating overlapping structures, while ensuring the separation of roles.

23.	The review identified efficiencies for the 2026/27 budget based on several central reforms to the Mission’s working methods. A core guiding principle of such a reform is that the centralization of authority for information collection, analysis and guidance would generate efficiencies through significant restructuring that yields a greater impact. The consolidation of workstreams, resulting in clearer guidance to all staff on their roles in an integrated process of data collection and analysis, would yield enhanced programme design and implementation. Together, the reforms would in turn produce more robust delivery, particularly in assuring the rights of non-majority communities, as well as in advocating for solutions on issues that favour progress in the Pristina-Belgrade dialogue.
24.	The proposed consolidation of workstreams consists of the following reforms:
(a)	Centralized data and information management by the Office of the Chief of Staff;
(b)	A peacekeeping intelligence mechanism and improved information flow between the Office of the Chief of Staff and the Joint Operations Centre/Joint Mission Analysis Centre;
(c)	Business Intelligence Unit by the Office of the Chief Mission Support;
(d)	Programme management oversight by the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General.

25.	The above reforms would enable an integrated cycle of data collection, analysis and guidance on programme design while furthering consistency and integrated impact analysis across offices in Pristina, Mitrovica, Peja/Pec and Belgrade. In that context, the transfer of a certain functions from the Mitrovica Regional Office to Pristina would strengthen the integration, as follows:
(a)	Integrating the analyses undertaken by the Mitrovica Regional Office and the Office of Community Support;

(b)	While still preserving a focus on the situation in the north, integrating analyses and collaboration between the Mitrovica Regional Office and the Human Rights Section would make it possible to assess the impact of political developments on all communities, ensuring consistent approaches that could better inform the protection of rights of non-majority communities;
(c)	Integrating a coordinated project management oversight over all projects and increasing the number and scope of projects undertaken by the Mission in key areas;
(d)	The oversight capacity would guide project design and implementation, while leaving implementation to those closest to the communities served;
(e)	Strengthening human rights analysis across the work of the Mission.

26.	UNMIK welcomed the recommendations of the civilian staffing review and proposes a number of staffing changes in the present report. UNMIK will use the results of the civilian staffing review exercise to assist in the redesigning of its structures, including the Mission support component, in line with the guidelines on mission support structure for small missions. By addressing staffing gaps and inadequacies and aligning support functions with the Mission’s evolving priorities and needs, the restructuring would maintain the Mission’s operational effectiveness and efficiency. The focus is on providing the required support in identifying the needs for the key workforce capabilities in data analysis and management, innovation and digital transformation.
D.	Regional mission cooperation

27.	During 2024/25 period, the Mission continued to strengthen its partnerships with key international partners in the area, including the Kosovo Force (KFOR), the European Union Rule of Law Mission in Kosovo (EULEX), the Office of the European Union Special Representative, the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) and the Council of Europe. In addition, the Mission worked with the United Nations Kosovo team within the framework of the integrated strategic framework for Kosovo for the period 2024–2026, including through the implementation of programmatic activities. The Mission also continued its collaboration with the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime through the implementation of the joint plan.

28.	The Mission also facilitated the activities of and its partnerships with the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, the United Nations Office for Project Services and the United Nations Human Settlements Programme, in accordance with the memorandums of understanding between UNMIK and the respective organizations. In addition, the Mission benefitted from the support of the Kuwait Joint Support Office, which embedded two staff members – one at the Field Support level and one at the General Service level – to process payroll for national staff and individual uniformed personnel, ensuring administrative efficiency and continuity.

29.	During the 2026/27 period, UNMIK will continue the same regional cooperation as in the 2024/25 period, apart from the cooperation with the Kuwait Joint Support Office. After the closure of the Kuwait Joint Support Office on 30 June 2026, the services thereafter would be provided by RSCE.
E.	Human resources
Executive direction and management

children.text: Component 1: substantive
Component 2: support
F.	Proposed staffing changes

30.	The civilian staffing review identified opportunities for efficiencies through workforce optimization, as described in paragraphs 21–26 of the present report. Consequently, it is proposed to: (a) abolish 49 posts, comprising 21 international posts (two D-1, two P-5, four P-4 and 13 Field Service level), two National Professional Officer posts and 26 national General Service posts; (b) establish one National Professional Officer post and two international United Nations Volunteer positions; (c) convert two Field Service posts to one National Professional Officer post and one national General Service post; (d) redeploy 31 posts, including five international posts, three National Professional Officer posts, 22 national General Service posts and one United Nations Volunteer position; and (e) reassign 24 posts, including seven international posts, one National Professional Officer post, 13 national General Service posts and three United Nations Volunteer positions. The details of the proposed staffing changes are presented in the table below.
Executive direction and management

31.	It is proposed that the Office of the Special Representative of Secretary-General strengthen its support of gender mainstreaming efforts by redeploying existing mission resources, through the following: (a) the reassignment of one position of Gender Officer (United Nations Volunteer) from the Office of Community Support; and (b) the redeployment of one post of Team Assistant (national General Service) from the Office of the Chief of Mission Support to provide support to the Gender Adviser (P-4) and the Associate Programme Management Officer (P-2) in implementing programmatic activities.

32.	As recommended in the civilian staffing review, it is proposed that the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General create a programme management function that would provide dedicated guidance, oversight, design and implementation coordination, monitoring and evaluation. It is proposed that the function would consist of one post of Programme Management Officer (P-3), one post of Assistant Programme Management Officer (National Professional Officer) and one position of Programme Management Officer (United Nations Volunteer), which would ensure optimal resourcing of the centralized function. It is also proposed to abolish one post of Senior Staff Assistant (Field Service) as an efficiency measure to establish shared administrative support between the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General and the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General.

33.	As recommended in the civilian staffing review, it is proposed that the Office of the Chief of Staff abolish one post of Team Assistant (national General Service) as an efficiency measure, which would optimize the existing structure, streamline administrative support functions and enhance the overall efficiency of the Mission. In addition, to strengthen the consolidation of cross-functional mission initiatives led by the Joint Operations Centre/Joint Mission Analysis Centre team and to ensure alignment with existing functional responsibilities and operational requirements, it is proposed that one post of Team Assistant (national General Service) be redeployed from the Office of the Chief of Staff to the Joint Operations Centre/Joint Mission Analysis Centre.

34.	The restructuring of the Human Rights Section is required in order to strengthen its capacity to provide additional support on various issues within the Mission’s footprint, including property rights, representation rights and other critical human rights issues. To regularize three internal temporary loans, it is proposed to redeploy to the Human Rights Section three national General Service posts, including one post of Administrative Assistant from the Office of Rule of Law, one post of Programme Management Assistant from the Mitrovica Regional Office and one post of Team Assistant from the Office of Community Support. It is also proposed to reclassify downwards one post of Human Rights Officer from the P-4 to the P-3 level, allowing UNMIK to focus on capacity-building and to rejuvenate the workforce by including lower-level posts.
Component 1: substantive

35.	As recommended in the civilian staffing review, it is proposed that the Office of Political Affairs strengthen political analysis and reporting to internal and external entities. The Mission has identified internal resources to address that need and proposes to reassign one post of Legal Officer (P-3) from the Office of Legal Affairs to the Office of Political Affairs, to perform the functions of Political Affairs Officer.

36.	The role of the Office of Rule of Law has evolved with the stronger capacities of Kosovo institutions and some overlaps with the mandate of EULEX. The civilian staffing review found the Office of Rule of Law was top-heavy, with multiple senior-level posts. Accordingly, the posts of Head of the Office of Rule of Law (D-1), Senior Rule of Law Officer (P-5) and Rule of Law Officer (P-4) are proposed to be abolished to reduce top-heavy structures, with an aim of rejuvenation. Furthermore, for optimal use of resources and additional efficiency, it is proposed to redeploy the Administrative Assistant post (national General Service) from the Office of Rule of Law to the Human Rights Section.

37.	For the Justice and Correction Section, the recommendations in the civilian staffing review are to ensure that the post titles reflect the actual duties, which comprise substantive rule of law governance and legal/judicial research and analysis. The Mission proposes to reassign two posts of Legal Officer (P-4) and one post of Assistant Legal Officer (National Professional Officer) as two posts of Rule of Law Officer (P-4) and one post of Assistant Rule of Law Officer (National Professional Officer), respectively, within the same Justice and Correction Section. As an efficiency measure, it is proposed to abolish one post of Corrections Officer (P-4) in the Section that has remained vacant for several years. Furthermore, it is proposed to redeploy one post of Administrative Assistant (national General Service) from the Office of the Senior Police Adviser to share administrative support.

38.	For the Office of the Senior Police Adviser, it is proposed to abolish one post of Administrative Assistant (Field Service) and one post of Programme Management Assistant (national General Service). The civilian staffing review identified that the functions performed under both posts could be performed under other posts, which would contribute to the streamlined structure and efficient use of resources.

39.	For the Office of Legal Affairs, as recommended in the civilian staffing review, it is proposed to abolish the post of Chief of Service, Legal Affairs (D-1). The abolishment supports the broader structural realignment to reduce senior-level positions in line with guidance on rejuvenation while optimizing resources and aligning the Mission’s organizational framework with its evolving operational requirements and mandate implementation priorities. In addition, it is proposed: (a) to abolish one post of Legal Officer (P-4) to streamline functions and ensure the optimal use of existing resources; and (b) to reassign one post of Legal Officer (P-3) to the Office of Political Affairs as a Political Affairs Officer to align with the priority functions of the Mission, given the reduced need for the services of the Legal Affairs Office. Furthermore, one post of Legal Officer (P-3) would be reassigned to the Office of the Deputy Special Representative of the Secretary-General to lead the new programmatic activities management function.

40.	In the Military Liaison Office, one post of Administrative Assistant (national General Service) is proposed for abolishment as an efficiency measure to streamline administrative functions within the Office.

41.	The Office of Strategic Communication and Public Affairs will aim to reinforce the strategic communications of the Mission on mission-wide issues and to ensure the specific needs of all communities are incorporated. Therefore, it is proposed to reassign one post of Civil Affairs Officer (P-3) from the Mitrovica Regional Office to the Office of Strategic Communication and Public Affairs as a Communications Officer to accurately reflect the post’s actual functions. In addition, one post of Communication Assistant (national General Service) is proposed to be reassigned as a Data Analysis Assistant within the Office, which would help to address the existing gap in data analysis functions.

42.	The United Nations Office in Belgrade, through the above-mentioned civilian staffing review, assessed its administrative support structure and identified redundancies and overlaps among Field Service and national General Service posts. Noting that the functions of one post of Administrative Officer (Field Service) and one post of Administrative Assistant (Field Service) were identified as redundant and could be performed under other posts, it is proposed that the posts be abolished in line with the efficiency efforts. This would also contribute to optimal and efficient structure of the Office while ensuring Professional and locally recruited staff are able to provide necessary support.

43.	The Office of Community Support, following the civilian staffing review, identified several restructuring opportunities to consolidate support for community activities across all municipalities and to ensure that the Office is structured optimally to support all communities. It is proposed to redeploy two posts of Civil Affairs Officer (one P-4 and one P-3) and one post of Associate Civil Affairs Officer (National Professional Officer) from the Mitrovica Regional Office to lead the Office of Community Support.

44.	The Civil Affairs Officer (P-4) would lead the field team in Pejë/Pec, as the only international staff present in the Pejë/Pec Office, which covers developments in 12 municipalities in western Kosovo through the conduct of regular field visits. With the proposed redeployment, the role would be aligned with the need to support that geographical area and with its current functional responsibilities under a temporary loan arrangement. The Civil Affairs Officer (P-3) would be a focal point for community support-related programmatic activities for the entire Mission and would ensure coverage for all communities. The Associate Civil Affairs Officer (National Professional Officer) would cover the mandatory mechanisms responsible for the protection and the promotion of the rights of non-majority communities. In recognition of the Mission’s priorities on human rights and gender mainstreaming, it is proposed that the Office of Community Support: (a) redeploy one post of Team Assistant (national General Service) to the Human Rights Section; and (b) reassign one position Civil Affairs Officer (United Nations Volunteer) as a Gender Officer, reporting to the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General, to support the Gender Adviser with substantive functions in supporting gender mainstreaming efforts.

45.	As recommended in the civilian staffing review, the following staffing changes are proposed for the Mitrovica Regional Office: (a) the redeployment of two posts of Civil Affairs Officer (one P-4 and one P-3) and one post of Associate Civil Affairs Officer (National Professional Officer) to the Office of Community Support; (b) the redeployment of one post of Programme Management Assistant (national General Service) to the Human Right Office; (c) the reassignment of one post of Civil Affairs Officer (P-3) as Communications Officer (P-3) to the Office of Strategic Communication and Public Affairs; (d) the reassignment two posts of Civil Affairs Officer (one P-4 and one P-3) to two posts of Political Affairs Officer within Mitrovica Regional Office in response to the evolving operational requirements in the substantive area; (e) the redeployment of two posts of Light Vehicle Driver (national General Service) to the Transport Unit, while remaining in Mitrovica; (f) the conversion of one post of Administrative Assistant from the Field Service to the national General Service category, as part of local capacity-building; (g) the abolishment of one post of Programme Management Assistant (national General Service); and (h) the reassignment of one position of Civil Affairs Officer (United Nations Volunteer) as Staff Counsellor, Mitrovica, reporting to the Medical Unit.
Component 2: support – Security Section

46.	The civilian staffing review of the Security Section identified that, given the stable security situation, eight posts are no longer required and, therefore, proposed for abolishment. To align the reporting lines with the level of responsibility of duties within the Section, it is proposed that one post of Chief Security Officer (P-3) be reclassified to a post of Associate Security Officer (P-2). In addition, in coordination with the Department of Safety and Security, to ensure that the standard nomenclature is applied consistently in UNMIK in line with other peacekeeping missions, it is proposed that three posts of Administrative Assistant (national General Service) (two in Pristina and one in Mitrovica) be reassigned as Field Security Assistant in the same locations, to align the functional titles with the actual functions being performed.
Mission Support Division

47.	As a result of the civilian staffing review, it is proposed that the Mission Support Division be restructured and realigned to ensure a more efficient and consolidated structure. This includes movement to a two-pillar structure, in line with the Department of Operational Support guidance on mission support structure for small missions, the realignment of posts to consolidate units and the transfer of technical functions across locations to report to the respective technical section/unit. The geographical location of the posts in Mitrovica and Belgrade will remain; however, the reporting lines of those posts that provide technical services will ensure unified oversight and accountability and maintain a consistent structure compared with other peacekeeping missions.

48.	As recommended in the civilian staffing review, the following staffing changes are proposed for the Office of the Chief Mission Support: (a) the redeployment of one post of Engineering Assistant, Mitrovica (national General Service), to report to the Facilities Management Unit; (b) the redeployment of two posts of Light Vehicle Driver, Belgrade (national General Service), to report to the Transport Unit; (c) the reassignment of one post of Telecommunications Technical Assistant, Belgrade (national General Service) to the Field Technology Section as Data Analysis Assistant, to reflect the actual functions performed under the post; (d) the reassignment of one position of Information System Officer, Mitrovica (United Nations Volunteer) to the Medical Unit as a Nurse, Mitrovica (United Nations Volunteer), retaining the Mitrovica location; and (e) the redeployment of one post of Team Assistant (national General Service) to the Office of the Special Representative of the Secretary-General.

49.	The creation of an Audit, Risk Management and Compliance Unit is proposed following the recommendation in the civilian staffing review. A consolidated stand-alone unit within the Office of the Chief of Mission Support would comprise the current Audit Response Cell, to which an Occupational Health and Safety, Environmental Cell and a Business Intelligence Unit would be added. Such a structure would ensure the Mission’s response to accountability and strategic oversight and would reduce direct reporting lines to the Chief of Mission Support. To create that Unit, it is proposed to redeploy: (a) one post of Associate Environment Officer (National Professional Officer) from the Environmental Cell; (b) one post of Associate Property Management Officer (P-2) and one position of Quality Assurance/Business Intelligence Officer (United Nations Volunteer) from the Supply Chain Performance Cell to provide an overview of data analysis of and indicators for entire Mission support functions; (c) one post of Logistics Assistant (national General Service) from the Mission Support Centre to carry out occupational health and safety functions and provide cross cutting support to the unit.
Operations and resource management pillar

50.	As recommended in the civilian staffing review, the Human Resources Section will focus on advisory functions, ensuring continued support to operational and managerial decision-making within the Mission. As an efficiency measure, and in line with the broader Secretariat-wide movement to consolidate administrative functions, it is proposed to transfer location-independent human resources transactional functions to RSCE. The Mission identified five posts for abolishment in order to reduce the number of UNMIK personnel and transfer functions to RSCE, thereby benefitting from greater efficiencies and effectiveness. To ensure capacity-building and utilize a more localized workforce, one post of Human Resources Assistant (Field Service) is proposed to be converted to an Assistant Human Resources Officer (National Professional Officer) post.

51.	Similarly, it is proposed that the Finance and Budget Section transfer its location-independent finance transactional functions to RSCE and abolish five posts, including two posts that were embedded in the Kuwait Joint Support Office (see para. 29 above).

52.	It is proposed to abolish four posts in the Field Technology Section. In addition, the reporting line for the post of Telecommunications Technical Assistant, Belgrade (national General Service) would be transferred to the Field Technology Section, while the post would be reassigned as a Data Analysis Assistant, Belgrade (national General Service). Also, the Administrative Assistant (national General Service) will be reassigned as Information Systems Assistant (national General Service) to reflect the actual functions to support the information and communication technology transformation projects.

53.	It is proposed to abolish the Mission Support Centre as a result of the restructuring of the Mission Support Division; two posts of Logistics Officer (Field Service) and Logistics Assistant (national General Service) would be redeployed.

54.	The Procurement Unit (five posts) will be redeployed to the operations and resource management pillar to mitigate procurement-related risk exposure from the merger of the supply chain management and the service delivery management pillars and to ensure that organizational units managing funds in service delivery are separated from the units performing procurement functions to avoid any conflict of interest and to enforce internal control.
Supply chain management and service delivery pillar

55.	It is proposed to merge the supply chain management and the service delivery management pillars into one pillar following the recommendation from the civilian staffing review and in line with the guidelines on mission support structure for small missions. The Chief, Supply Chain Management (P-5) would take over the leadership of the consolidated pillar to optimize the existing resources and increase overall efficiency, while the Chief, Service Delivery (P-5) post would be abolished to optimize the structure by reducing the top-heavy nature of the Mission Support Division.

56.	The civilian staffing review identified the need for the following adjustments in the Medical Unit: (a) the reclassification of the post of Chief Medical Officer (P-4) to Medical Officer (P-3), to allow the efficient use of resources while maintaining the effectiveness of medical oversight and services within the Mission; (b) the reassignment of the position of Nurse, Mitrovica (United Nations Volunteer) from the Office of the Chief Mission Support to address the critical need for Medical Unit support in the provision of medical services in nursing, including injections, administering intravenous medication, disinfection and sterilization of medical equipment, triage, patient escort and other nursing services in Mitrovica; (c) the reassignment of the position of Civil Affairs Officer (United Nations Volunteer) from the Mitrovica Regional Office to the Medical Unit as Staff Counsellor, Mitrovica (United Nations Volunteer) based in Mitrovica; and (d) the establishment of the position of Staff Counsellor (United Nations Volunteer) based in Pristina in the Medical Unit, while the post of Staff Counsellor (P-4) and the Office of the Staff Counsellor will be abolished.

57.	The proposed staffing changes in the Transport Unit aim to achieve efficiencies through consolidation of the various functions, as follows: (a) the abolishment of two national General Service posts as the functions are no longer required owing to the overall decrease in the workload for the Unit; (b) the redeployment of two national General Service posts based in Belgrade and two national General Service posts based in Mitrovica to the Transport Unit, while retaining in their geographic location; and (c) the redeployment of one national General Service post to the newly created consolidated Logistics Support Unit.

58.	It is proposed to strengthen Facilities Management Unit through: (a) the reassignment of one post of Administrative Assistant (national General Service) as Facilities Management Assistant to align the functional title with the actual role and tasks performed under the post; and (b) the redeployment of one post of Engineering Assistant, Mitrovica (national General Service) to the Facilities Management Unit to ensure the necessary technical skills to support the Mitrovica Regional Office.

59.	As part of the restructuring and consolidation efforts across the Mission Support Division, and to reflect the actual functions performed by the unit, the Supply Chain Performance Cell will be renamed the Property Management Unit with the following staffing adjustments: (a) the abolishment of two posts to reduce redundancy; (b) the redeployment of one post and one position to the newly created Audit, Risk Management and Compliance Unit.

60.	Following the merger of the supply chain management and the service delivery management pillars, it is proposed to merge, consolidate and abolish redundant several small units and cells, namely, the Centralized Warehousing Unit, the Acquisition Management Unit and the Movement Control Unit into one Logistics Support Unit that will cover the end-to-end range of logistics functions, given the relatively small logistics support needs of UNMIK. The Logistics Support Unit will be part of the combined supply chain management and service delivery pillar and led by the Logistics Officer (Field Service) redeployed from the Mission Support Centre. The other 13 national General Service posts will be redeployed from the Acquisition Management Unit (five posts), the Centralized Warehousing Unit (seven posts) and the Transport Unit (one post). The remaining five posts in the Centralized Warehousing Unit and one long-vacant post in the Acquisition Management Unit are to be abolished. The Movement Control Assistant (national General Service) post in Movement Control is proposed to be abolished; residual functions will be carried out by the newly established Logistics Support Unit.

children.text: G.	Results-based budgeting frameworks
Component 1: substantive

table_content.text: 1.1	Progress towards reconciliation and integration of all communities in Kosovo

1.1.1	Increase in the number of activities initiated and/or undertaken by municipal institutions, as well as by civil society and local organizations, promoting intercommunity trust-building and integration in municipalities throughout Kosovo (2022/23: 927; 2023/24: 1,260; 2024/25: 930; 2025/26: 935; 2026/27: 940)

1.1.2	Human rights initiatives from the 2023 United Nations Kosovo Trust-building Forum are implemented by the Kosovo institutions, municipalities and civil society across Kosovo (2022/23: 12; 2023/24: 13; 2024/25: 17; 2025/26: 15; 2026/27: 15)
1.2	Progress with respect to Pristina’s cooperation and dialogue with Belgrade and regional organizations

1.2.1	Increase in the number of civil cases resolved in the Mitrovica Basic Court and its Branches in Leposavić/Leposaviq and Zubin Potok, following completion of integration of the judiciary, in accordance with the agreements reached in the European Union-facilitated dialogue (2022/23: 4,960; 2023/24: 3,243; 2024/25: 3,275; 2025/26: 2,100; 2026/27: 2,200)

1.2.2	Increase in the number of meetings between Pristina and Belgrade representatives of the Working Group on persons who are unaccounted for in connection with events in Kosovo, and at the sub-Working Group levels (2022/23: n/a; 2023/24: 1; 2024/25: 4; 2025/26: 5; 2026/27: 6)

1.2.3	Facilitate the locally driven initiatives to integrate gender perspectives in the normalization process and the European Union-facilitated dialogue between Belgrade and Pristina officials (2022/23: n/a; 2023/24: n/a; 2024/25: n/a; 2025/2026: n/a; 2026/2027: 4)
1.3	Progress with regard to the rule of law, security and Human rights

1.3.1	Increase in the satisfaction level of respondents in public perception surveys on the judiciary (2022/23: 24.4 per cent; 2023/24: 30 per cent; 2024/25: 27 per cent); 2025/26: 36 per cent; 2026/27: 37 per cent)

1.3.3	Kosovo authorities demonstrate accountability for human rights by implementing an increased percentage of recommendations of the Ombudsperson Institution of Kosovo (2022/23: 27 per cent; 2023/24: 17 per cent; 2024/25: 17 per cent; 2025/26: 58 per cent; 2026/27: 18 per cent)

Note: The amounts represent the total obligations that were cancelled at the end of the 12-month period following the end of the budget period in respect of goods supplied and services rendered in the budget period, and at the end of an additional period of four years in respect of obligations to troop- and police-contributing countries.
F.	Other revenue and adjustments, and borrowing
1.	Other revenue and adjustments
(Thousands of United States dollars)
a	Breakdown of other/miscellaneous revenue: refund of prior-period expenses ($4,800); and other revenue ($13,700).

67.	Credits comprising the unencumbered balances, other revenue and the cancellation of prior-period obligations reduce the assessed contributions owed by Member States in the following financial year. The credit for the 2024/25 period for overall peacekeeping operations amounts to $335.5 million, including the support entities and MINUSMA. In the current financial situation, offsetting this large amount of credit against a future assessment for the 2026/27 period will reduce the collection and undermine the ability of peacekeeping operations to fully implement their mandates. Accordingly, the Secretary-General proposes that the General Assembly approve that the credits in respect of the financial period 2024/25 be placed in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the implementation of those mandates and allow Member States to apply their share towards current or future dues once the Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has sufficiently improved. This proposal is explained in more detail in the peacekeeping overview report.
2.	Borrowing
68.	Owing to its cash liquidity situation, the Mission received loans from the Peacekeeping Reserve Fund and other active peacekeeping missions, with the month-end loan balance as follows:
(Millions of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Peacekeeping Reserve Fund (22.6), UNIFIL (4.3), UNDOF (3.8), MONUSCO (3.4)

children.text: Abbreviations: UNIFIL, United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon; UNDOF, United Nations Disengagement Observer Force; MONUSCO, United Nations Organization Stabilization Mission in the Democratic Republic of the Congo.
G.	Vacancy factors
69.	The cost estimates for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027 take into account the following vacancy factors:
(Percentage)

70.	The vacancy factors take into account the high number of abolishments included in the proposed budget for 2026/27. Accordingly, the proposed vacancy rates for civilian personnel categories with abolishments were estimated using the total number of vacant posts and positions as at 31 December 2025, net of the number of posts and positions proposed for abolishment. For categories without proposed abolishments, the standard policy guidance was applied, using either the 12-month average vacancy rate from January to December 2025 or the actual vacancy rate as at 31 December 2025. For military and police personnel, the vacancy factors reflect their planned deployment during the period. This approach ensures that vacancy rates are based on, to the extent possible, the actual data while maintaining consistency across the proposed budgets.
H.	Programmatic activities
71.	The estimated resource requirements for programmatic activities for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, compared with previous periods, are as follows:
(Thousands of United States dollars)

table_content.text: Rule of law and security sector reform
Promotion and protection of human rights

children.text: 72.	Detailed information on programmatic activities undertaken during the 2024/25 period, as well as on activities planned for the 2026/27 period, is provided in the supplementary information. The resource requirements for the performance and budget periods are for projects implemented by the Mission to support mandate implementation as outlined in the Mission’s results-based budgeting framework. The proposed increase in resources for programmatic activities will strengthen the Mission’s ability to build on past successes and respond more effectively to changing political dynamics in the Mission area.
73.	The estimated resource requirements for quick-impact projects for the period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027, compared with previous periods, are as follows:
(Thousands of United States dollars)

74.	UNMIK will implement the quick-impact projects as a confidence-building measure in support of mandate implementation and will enhance their impact on the community, in line with the General Assembly resolution 76/274. As part of building trust between UNMIK and local communities, the Mission is proposing projects that are focused mainly on small infrastructure interventions to address community needs in multi-ethnic areas and areas of contact between communities, for the projects to have the greatest impact. Where possible, quick-impact projects will include elements related to cross-cutting agendas such as human rights; women and peace and security; environmental protection; youth, peace and security; and the well-being of persons with special requirements. The proposed increase in quick-impact projects will enable the Mission to be more responsive to the changing political dynamics in the Mission area, thereby placing UMIK in a stronger position to advance its trust-building strategy more effectively.

75.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the application of the higher revised mission subsistence allowance rate, effective 1 January 2025, offset in part by the fewer-than-planned travel on emplacement and rotation.

76.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the application of: (a) the higher vacancy rate of 12.5 per cent compared with the zero vacancy rate applied in the approved budget; and (b) the lower revised mission subsistence allowance rate effective 1 January 2026.

77.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily to the application of the higher revised mission subsistence allowance rate, effective 1 January 2025, offset in part by the higher actual average vacancy rate of 10 per cent compared with the budgeted full incumbency.

78.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the application of: (a) the higher vacancy rate of 20.0 per cent compared with the zero vacancy rate applied in the approved budget; and (b) the lower revised mission subsistence allowance rate effective 1 January 2026.

79.	For the 2024/25 period, the increased requirements were attributable primarily due to the lower actual vacancy rate of 10.6 per cent versus the rate of 16.8 per cent applied in the approved budget.

80.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to the proposed abolishment of 21 international posts, the downward reclassification of three posts (two P-4 to P-3 and one P-3 to P-2) and the conversion of two international posts to national posts, as recommended in the civilian staffing review. The reduced requirements are offset in part by the application of the lower vacancy rate of 1.1 per cent compared with 8.8 per cent applied in the approved budget.

81.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the actual average vacancy rate of 6.1 per cent compared with the budgeted full incumbency; (b) the appreciation of the United States dollar against the euro, with an actual average rate of 0.920 euros to one United States dollar, compared with the budgeted rate of 0.912; and (c) the lower actual requirement for family allowance.

82.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of the higher vacancy rate of 12.1 per cent, compared with the zero vacancy rate applied in the approved budget; (b) the net impact of the proposed abolishment of two posts, the establishment of one post and the conversion of one international post, as recommended in the civilian staffing review. The reduced requirements are offset in part by the application of: (a) the higher salary scale effective 1 December 2024 and promulgated in July 2025; and (b) the exchange rate of 0.863 euros to one United States dollar versus the rate of 0.948 applied in the approved budget.

83.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to the proposed establishment of two international United Nations Volunteer positions of Staff Counsellor and Programme Management Officer, as recommended in the civilian staffing review.

84.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for international staff, through: (a) the lower actual costs for individual communication and information technology consultants; and (b) the deferral of the planned transition from ISO 27001:2013 to ISO 27001:2022 information security management systems certification and the development and implementation of ISO 22301:2019 business continuity systems certification.

85.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable to the absence of airfare provisions for the individual information and communication technology consultants, owing to the new contractual arrangements.

86.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for international staff, through the cancellation of travel for a number of annual conferences, for consultations with Headquarters counterparts and for rule of law-related meetings, as well as fewer trips to Belgrade for political consultations and the cancellation of travel to a number of in-person workshops and trainings.

87.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for international staff, through the deferral of the following budgeted projects: (a) the replacement of a concrete path and construction of a fire exit to ensure compliance with occupational safety and health requirements in the United Nations Office in Belgrade; (b) minor security improvement works; (c) the replacement of the existing diesel heating plant in the medical facility to continue improvements to the heating systems of the Mission; and (d) the construction of structures for the installation of the solar-powered system at the Mission headquarters in Pristina.

88.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the application of the exchange rate of 0.863 euros to one United States dollar versus the rate of 0.948 applied in the approved budget; and (b) the new requirement for the rental of premises for the Mitrovica Regional Office, including the costs of relocation to the new office.

89.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for international staff through the deferred acquisition of two vehicles scheduled for replacement; and (b) the lower-than-budgeted level of fuel consumption, namely, 32,400 litres of diesel and 13,300 litres of petrol, compared with the budgeted 53,000 litres of diesel and 22,000 litres of petrol.

90.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for international staff, through the deferral of: (a) acquisitions of software and licences and spare parts to carry out repairs of communication and information technology equipment; (b) some publication and information activities in connection with community outreach initiatives; and (c) the digitalization of the Mission’s historical audiovisual archives.

91.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) the provisions for five information and communication technology contractors, compared with eight contactors provisioned in the approved budget; (b) the lower rates for centrally managed communications and information technology services; and (c) the lower number of licenses due to the proposed staffing reduction recommended in the civilian staffing review.

92.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to: (a) the absence of requirements for medical evacuation by budgeted charter aircraft and for mortuary services; and (b) the fewer-than-budgeted acquisition of vaccines and medical supplies due to the availability of stock.

93.	For the 2026/27 period, the reduced requirements are attributable primarily to: (a) no requirement for the replacement of defibrillators; and (b) the lower acquisition of medical supplies due to availability of supplies in stock.

94.	For the 2024/25 period, the reduced requirements were attributable primarily to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for international staff, through: (a) the deferral of acquisitions, resulting in reduced requirements for freight and related costs; and (b) the cancellation of a number of trainings, resulting in reduced requirements for training fees.

95.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements under programmatic activities are attributable primarily to the requirement to strengthen the Mission’s capacity to build on past successes and adapt to changing dynamics in the Mission area in three specific ways. First, the additional resources will enable UNMIK to scale up trust-building programmatic activities that have proved effective and scalable. Second, the increase in resources will allow the Mission to provide critical support to the Barabar Centre so it can broaden the reach of its work. Third, the additional resources will strengthen the Mission’s capacity to be nimble in its programming response to the new political dynamics, including those emerging in northern Kosovo, in particular, as a result of recent elections.

96.	For the 2026/27 period, the increased requirements are attributable primarily to additional requirements for quick-impact projects that focus on small-scale infrastructure interventions intended to address community needs in multi-ethnic areas and areas of contact between communities, where such projects are expected to have the greatest impact.

text: IV.	Actions to be taken by the General Assembly

children.text: 97.	The actions to be taken by the General Assembly in connection with the financing of the United Nations Interim Administration Mission in Kosovo are:
(a)	To take note of the unencumbered balance of $25,100 with respect to the period from 1 July 2024 to 30 June 2025;

(b)	To take note of other revenue for the period ended 30 June 2025 amounting to $81,000 from investment revenue ($100), other/miscellaneous revenue ($18,500) and the cancellation of prior-period obligations ($62,400);

(c)	To approve the apportionment of the amount of $106,200 among Member States in accordance with the applicable scale of assessment for 2025 and to place such apportioned amount in a reserve that would be used in the event that collections are insufficient to enable the full, efficient and effective implementation of mandates;

(d)	To allow Member States to apply their share of the amount apportioned in subparagraph (c) above towards current or future dues once the General Assembly determines that the liquidity situation has improved sufficiently to allow such application;
(e)	To appropriate the amount $42,022,400 for the maintenance of the Mission for the 12-month period from 1 July 2026 to 30 June 2027.

text: V.	Summary of follow-up action taken to implement the decisions and requests of the General Assembly in its resolutions 76/274 and 79/305, including the requests and recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions endorsed by the Assembly

children.text: A.	General Assembly
Cross-cutting issues
(Resolution 76/274)

table_content.text: Requests the Secretary-General to improve comprehensive oversight of the activities of peacekeeping missions and implement the recommendations of relevant oversight bodies in this regard to avoid deficiencies in management and related economic losses, with the aim of ensuring full compliance with the Financial Regulations and Rules of the United Nations (para. 17).

As at 31 December 2025, six of the seven recommendations (86 per cent) issued by the Office of Internal Oversight Services had been implemented; subsequently, as at 16 January 2026, 100 per cent of the recommendations issued by the Office of Internal Oversight and 100 per cent of the recommendations issued by the Board of Auditors had been implemented. Regular follow-ups are undertaken to ensure the timely closure of the remaining recommendations.

Requests that future budget proposals demonstrate the scalability of mission support components, including their staffing and operational costs, be proportionate in relation to the changing level of other mission components and include standard indicators (para. 27).
The mission support ratio (total number of mission support and security personnel per 1,000 total mission personnel) in the 2025/26 period is 481, which is the same 481 as in the 2020/21 period.

Requests the Secretary-General to consider options for greater nationalization of functions when formulating budget submissions, commensurate with mission mandates and requirements where applicable (para. 34).

As part of the civilian staffing review conducted for UNMIK in 2025/26, the options for localization were considered and several opportunities to convert international posts to national posts were proposed as part of the present budget report.

Recalls the provisions of section XVIII of its resolution 61/276, recognizes the important role played by quick-impact projects in establishing and building confidence in missions, further recognizes the importance of conducting assessments of the quick-impact projects regularly, on their needs and impact as required in resolution 61/276, requests the Secretary-General to include the conducted assessments in his next reports, and also requests the Secretary-General to enhance their impact (para. 82).

A new provision of $200,000 for the implementation of four quick-impact projects was included in the report of the Secretary-General for the 2025/26 period. UNMIK will undertake an assessment as part of the review of the first set of quick-impact projects.

children.text: B.	Advisory Committee on Administrative and Budgetary Questions
Financing of the United Nations Administration Mission in Kosovo
(A/79/724/Add.4 and General Assembly resolution 79/305)

table_content.text: The Advisory Committee notes that the 2025/26 period will be the first time in which quick-impact projects will be implemented by the Mission and trusts that UNMIK will ensure that the projects benefit the local population, as intended, including through hard-walled projects that will leave a positive legacy of the Mission in the local communities. The Committee further trusts that projects will be implemented in accordance with existing policies and guidelines and that updated information, including on planning, implementation and assessment, will be provided in the context of the next proposed budget report (para. 24).
UNMIK has begun implementation of quick-impact projects in line with the guidance from the General Assembly and will provide further information on implementation as the projects proceed.

The Advisory Committee notes the ongoing efforts of the Mission to improve its environmental footprint and trusts that further efficiency gains will be generated from the photovoltaic power plants, as well as other environmental initiatives, and will be reflected in future reports (para. 27).

The installation of the solar powered system at the Mission headquarters in Pristina was deferred due to the reprioritization of resources to cover the increased requirements for civilian personnel. The efficiencies generated by the Mission through cost avoidance are reflected in the table for efficiency gains (see para. 64).

The Advisory Committee recalls General Assembly resolution 76/274 and considers that, as a matter of principle, the Secretary-General should ensure the continuous optimization of the staffing structures, including through civilian and security staffing reviews, with the aim to identify efficiencies that will be reflected in the next budget proposal (para. 28).

The civilian staffing review for UNMIK was undertaken in 2025/26 in line with General Assembly resolution 76/274. The present report includes the proposed staffing changes resulting from the recommendations of the civilian staffing review.
